script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. Thursday — here is your SOXX recap. The portfolio closed zero point two nine percent down on the session.

Intel just reported its fastest revenue growth in nearly fifteen years, and the afterhours reaction is rewriting the narrative for every major semiconductor name in this portfolio.

The Nasdaq composite led a broad market sell-off Thursday, dragged lower by escalating tensions in the Iran-U.S. conflict that rattled risk sentiment across equities. No major Federal Reserve speakers or economic prints were scheduled, leaving geopolitics as the primary pressure valve. The Nasdaq closed at a four-week low, with the session's losses spread wide across sectors, leaving chip stocks caught in the crossfire despite fundamentally improving demand signals.

Intel's second quarter results arrived after the close Thursday and they were not close — they were a blowout. The company reported adjusted earnings per share of forty-two cents against a Wall Street consensus of twenty-one cents, doubling expectations. Revenue came in at sixteen point one billion dollars versus estimates of fourteen point four three billion, representing the company's fastest top-line growth in nearly fifteen years. CFRA Research senior vice president and equity analyst Angelo Zino noted that Intel's PC business trajectory will increasingly be dictated by artificial intelligence data center momentum — a signal that enterprise demand is broadening beyond hyperscaler orders. The print sent Intel surging more than eleven percent in extended trading, triggering an immediate halo effect across the semiconductor space, with Advanced Micro Devices and Arm Holdings both gaining in afterhours. The read-through here is straightforward: if the server CPU market is accelerating at this pace, multiple names in this portfolio are sitting directly in the demand stream.

Advanced Micro Devices was down two point two nine percent in Thursday's session, but the afterhours picture is sharply different. The company's own Advancing AI conference delivered a keynote from chair and chief executive Lisa Su, who unveiled the Helios platform — a full rack-scale artificial intelligence system she called, in her words, "simply the best AI rack in the world." That followed a separate announcement of a partnership with Cerebras, pairing their technologies for AI systems deployment. On top of that, the Intel earnings halo effect lifted Advanced Micro Devices in extended hours, with analysts noting that a strong Intel server CPU quarter raises the revenue ceiling for competitors operating in the same demand environment. Watch the Advancing AI conference follow-up commentary closely.

Micron Technology was up three point two zero percent Thursday, one of the session's strongest performers in this portfolio. Alphabet's earnings report and capital expenditure guidance drove gains in high-bandwidth memory suppliers, with Micron and SK Hynix both rising on the read-through that hyperscaler memory demand remains robust. That is a clean, direct catalyst. Watch Micron's own forward guidance commentary for confirmation.

Nvidia was down one point five six percent on the session. One analyst piece noted that despite Alphabet's results providing fuel for a chip sector rally, Nvidia was notably left behind. That relative underperformance is worth monitoring. Broadcom was down one point zero nine percent. No specific news drove the move — broad sector pressure weighed. Turning to Intel, which was down two point three three percent during Thursday's regular session before the afterhours reversal of more than eleven percent on the earnings beat described in the top story. That afterhours move is the dominant development for this holding.

Applied Materials was up one point six zero percent Thursday, outperforming the broader sector sell-off. No company-specific headlines drove the move, but strength in memory-related capital equipment demand, consistent with the Micron and Alphabet read-through, likely provided support. Watch for any equipment order commentary tied to high-bandwidth memory capacity expansion. KLA Corporation was up one point eight eight percent, the strongest session performer among the primary holdings Thursday. Like Applied Materials, no single headline catalyzed the move, but equipment names held up well against the broader tape. Any confirmation of expanding wafer fabrication orders would be a direct catalyst.

Marvell Technology was down zero point seven nine percent Thursday. No company-specific news moved the stock, and relative to the broader Nasdaq weakness, the decline was contained. Upcoming data center networking demand data will be key to watch. Lam Research was up zero point one five percent — essentially flat against a difficult tape. No specific headlines, but resilience in a down session is notable given the broader Nasdaq pressure. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing was down one point three four percent Thursday. No company-specific news drove the move, but macro risk-off sentiment tied to geopolitical tensions — including the Iran-U.S. conflict cited in broader market coverage — historically weighs on names with significant geographic concentration exposure.

Three specific items to track in the next forty-eight hours: First, Intel's earnings call commentary on data center backlog and server CPU guidance, which will directly inform the demand outlook for multiple names in this portfolio. Second, any follow-up analyst notes from the Advanced Micro Devices Advancing AI conference, particularly around Helios platform customer commitments and revenue timing. Third, watch for additional hyperscaler capital expenditure disclosures, as each major cloud earnings report this season resets the demand floor for semiconductor equipment and memory names.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for SOXX. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. The key question heading into Friday is whether Intel's afterhours surge translates into a sustained sector-wide lift when regular trading opens.
